To manually set the desired margins for the size of the box, adjust the size expression with margins as you did before: ```javascript // Calculate the box size with the desired margin var boxWidth = textRect.width + (margin * 2); // Add margin to the left and right var boxHeight = textRect.height + (margin * 2); // Add margin to the top and bottom [boxWidth, boxHeight] ``` Now you have two sets of variables (`manualAdjustX` and `manualAdjustY`) that you can tweak to fine-tune the final position of the box. If you find that you frequently need to adjust these values, you might consider using expression controls like "Slider Control" to create a user interface for these adjustments directly in After Effects. To use sliders for the manual adjustments: 1. Select the box layer and choose `Effect` > `Expression Controls` > `Slider Control`. Rename the effect to "Adjust X" or similar. 2. Duplicate the Slider Control effect and rename the second one to "Adjust Y" or similar. 3. Link the `manualAdjustX` and `manualAdjustY` variables to these sliders by using the pick whip or the following expression code snippet: ```javascript var manualAdjustX = thisLayer.effect("Adjust X")("Slider"); var manualAdjustY = thisLayer.effect("Adjust Y")("Slider"); ``` Now you'll have an interface to adjust the manual X and Y position values directly within the Effect Controls panel for that layer. Make sure to adjust the sliders to fine-tune the final position as needed.

Amendment to Title 17, United States Code** Title 17, United States Code, is amended as follows: **Section 3. Exception for Derived Photographic Works of Three-Dimensional Craftsmanship** (a) Section [NEW] is added to Chapter 1, Subchapter A of Title 17 to read as follows: **§ [NEW]. (2) For purposes of this section, a "three-dimensional work of craftsmanship" includes sculptures, statues, and other artistic objects of three-dimensional nature protected under copyright law. (3) This section shall not be construed to limit any other rights or exceptions under this title, including but not limited to fair use. **Section 4. Exception for Limited Use of Frames or Title Cards from Audiovisual Works** (a) Section [NEW] is added to Chapter 1, Subchapter A of Title 17 to read as follows: **§ [NEW]. Limited Use of Single Frames or Title Cards from Audiovisual Works for Commentary, Analysis, or Discussion** (1) No violation of copyright shall arise from the use of a single frame or single title card from a longer audiovisual work if all of the following conditions are met: (A) The use is solely to identify, comment on, or provide context for the audiovisual work in commentary, criticism, or discussion; (B) The quality of the reproduced single frame or title card does not exceed standard definition resolution; and (C) The use is limited to a single frame or single title card per audiovisual work. (2) For purposes of this section, "audiovisual work" has the meaning given in section 101 of this title. (3) This section does not limit fair use or other exceptions provided under this title. **Section 5. Effective Date** This Act shall take effect 180 days after enactment. --- **Notes & Commentary:** - The exemption for “single derived photographic work” is modeled to balance the rights of copyright holders with public interest in photographing three-dimensional artworks displayed publicly. - The audiovisual frame exemption aims at enabling commentary and analysis without needing permission for incidental stills at limited resolution, akin to a fair use safe harbor. - This draft maintains existing fair use rights and does not grant carte blanche use beyond the narrowly defined exceptions. - Additional clarifications and definitions could be added based on intent and scope. USER: what is the difference between $A$6 and A$6 in excel functions

LLM: In Excel, the use of the dollar sign (`$`) in references is related to creating absolute references. It determines how a reference behaves when you copy the formula across cells. There are three types of references you can create: 1. **Relative Reference (e.g., `A6`)**: When you copy a formula with a relative reference, both the row and column in the reference change relative to the position where you move the formula. For example, if you copy a formula from cell B1 to C1, a reference like `A6` would automatically adjust to `B6`. 2. **Absolute Reference (e.g., `$A$6`)**: Both the column (`A`) and the row (`6`) are fixed, meaning they do not change when you copy the formula to another cell. A reference like `$A$6` will always refer to cell A6, no matter where the formula is copied. 3. **Mixed Reference (e.g., `A$6` or `$A6`)**: Only one of the two components (either row or column) is fixed. - `A$6`: The row (6) is fixed, but the column (A) can change. When copied across rows, the reference will stay row 6. However, if copied across columns, the column changes relative to the move (e.g., copying from B1 to C1 will change `A$6` to `B$6`). - `$A6`: The column (A) is fixed, but the row (6) can change. When copied across columns, the column remains the same, but if copied across rows, the row number would change (e.g., copying from B1 to B2 would change `$A6` to `$A7`). To summarize, `A$6` locks the row reference and allows the column reference to change, while `$A6` locks the column reference and allows the row reference to change.

If no separator is specified, then it should separate the strings by spaces: print(concat("turtle")) # "turtle" print(concat("cat", "dog")) # "cat dog" print(concat("a", "b", "c", sep=":")) # "a:b:c" You do not need to call a function, just implement it. LLM: To implement the `concat` function that takes an arbitrary number of strings and combines them using a specified separator, you can use the `*args` feature to collect all positional arguments into a tuple, and a keyword argument `sep` to specify the separator. By default, the separator will be a space. Here's the function: ```python def concat(*args, sep=" "): return sep.join(args) # Example usage: print(concat("turtle")) # Output: "turtle" print(concat("cat", "dog")) # Output: "cat dog" print(concat("a", "b", "c", sep=":")) # Output: "a:b:c" ``` ### Explanation: - **`*args`**: This allows the function to accept any number of arguments (positional), which are collected into a tuple named `args`. - **`sep=" "`**: This keyword argument sets the default separator to a space. It can be overridden by passing a different separator when calling the function. - **`sep.join(args)`**: Utilizes the `join` method of strings to concatenate the elements in `args` with the specified separator `sep`. This solution will correctly join any number of input strings using either the default space separator or a specified one.

LLM: To implement the `average_mark` function that calculates the average of a varying number of integer test scores, you can use the `*args` feature to collect all the integer inputs into a tuple. You'll calculate the average and round it to one decimal place using the `round` function. Here's the code: ```python def average_mark(*args): if len(args) == 0: return 0.0 # Alternatively, you could handle this situation differently, e.g., raise an exception total = sum(args) average = total / len(args) return round(average, 1) # The function should not be called here, as per the instructions. ``` ### Explanation: - **`*args`**: This allows the function to accept any number of integer arguments, which are collected into a tuple named `args`. - **Check for zero arguments**: If no arguments are provided, the function returns `0.0`. You can also choose to raise an exception if that better fits your needs. - **`sum(args)`**: Calculates the sum of all the integers provided as input. - **`len(args)`**: Gets the count of input integers. - **Average calculation**: Divides the sum by the count to get the average. - **`round(average, 1)`**: Rounds the result to one decimal place as required. This function will compute the desired average of the given marks correctly.

LLM: Let's start by implementing the `Book`, `TextBook`, and `Novel` classes as described. Then, we will create the `BookTester` class to demonstrate the functionality. ### Book Class ```java public class Book { private String title; private String author; public Book(String title, String author) { this.title = title; this.author = author; } public String getTitle() { return title; } public void setTitle(String title) { this.title = title; } public String getAuthor() { return author; } public void setAuthor(String author) { this.author = author; } @Override public String toString() { return "Book: " + title; } } ``` ### TextBook Class ```java public class TextBook extends Book { private int edition; private boolean isEbook; public TextBook(String title, String author, int edition, boolean isEbook) { super(title, author); this.edition = edition; this.isEbook = isEbook; } public int getEdition() { return edition; } public void setEdition(int edition) { this.edition = edition; } public boolean isEbook() { return isEbook; } public void setEbook(boolean isEbook) { this.isEbook = isEbook; } @Override public String toString() { return "Textbook edition # " + edition; } } ``` ### Novel Class ```java public class Novel extends Book { private int readingLevel; private String genre; public Novel(String title, String author, int readingLevel, String genre) { super(title, author); this.readingLevel = readingLevel; this.genre = genre; } public int getReadingLevel() { return readingLevel; } public void setReadingLevel(int readingLevel) { this.readingLevel = readingLevel; } public String getGenre() { return genre; } public void setGenre(String genre) { this.genre = genre; } @Override public String toString() { return genre + " Novel"; } } ``` ### BookTester Class ```java import java.util.ArrayList; public class BookTester { public static void main(String[] args) { // Create an ArrayList to store books ArrayList<Book> library = new ArrayList<>(); // Add textbooks to the library library.add(new TextBook("Introduction to Algorithms", "Thomas H. Cormen", 3, false)); library.add(new TextBook("Java Programming", "Joyce Farrell", 9, true)); library.add(new TextBook("Physics for Scientists and Engineers", "Raymond A. Serway", 8, false)); // Add novels to the library library.add(new Novel("To Kill a Mockingbird", "Harper Lee", 8, "Fiction")); library.add(new Novel("1984", "George Orwell", 9, "Dystopian")); library.add(new Novel("The Hobbit", "J.R.R. Tolkien", 7, "Fantasy")); // Print out information for each book in the library for (Book book : library) { System.out.println(book); if (book instanceof TextBook) { TextBook textBook = (TextBook) book; System.out.println("Title: " + textBook.getTitle()); System.out.println("Author: " + textBook.getAuthor()); System.out.println("Edition: " + textBook.getEdition()); System.out.println("Is eBook: " + textBook.isEbook()); } else if (book instanceof Novel) { Novel novel = (Novel) book; System.out.println("Title: " + novel.getTitle()); System.out.println("Author: " + novel.getAuthor()); System.out.println("Reading Level: " + novel.getReadingLevel()); System.out.println("Genre: " + novel.getGenre()); } System.out.println(); } } } ``` ### Explanation 1. **Book Class**: - Contains `title` and `author` instance variables. - Includes a constructor, getters, and setters for these variables. - Provides a `toString` method to print the title of the book. 2. **TextBook Class**: - Inherits from `Book`. - Adds `edition` and `isEbook` instance variables. - Includes a constructor, getters, and setters for the new variables. - Provides an overridden `toString` method to print the edition of the textbook. 3. **Novel Class**: - Inherits from `Book`. - Adds `readingLevel` and `genre` instance variables. - Includes a constructor, getters, and setters for the new variables. - Provides an overridden `toString` method to print the genre of the novel. 4. **BookTester Class**: - Creates an `ArrayList` of `Book` objects. - Adds three `TextBook` objects and three `Novel` objects to the list. - Iterates over the list and prints out the information about each book, using the appropriate type-specific methods. Running the `BookTester` class will output the information for each book stored in the library.

LLM: Let's go through each class and implement the required constructors and methods. We'll start with the `Solid` class, which you have partially provided, and go from there. ### Solid Class The `Solid` class should have the `name` instance variable, as well as methods for `volume` and `surfaceArea`, which should be overridden in the subclasses. ```java public class Solid { private String myName; public Solid(String name) { myName = name; } public String getName() { return myName; } // This should be overridden in the subclass public double volume() { return 0; } // This should be overridden in the subclass public double surfaceArea() { return 0; } } ``` ### Pyramid Class The Pyramid class will represent a rectangular pyramid. ```java import java.lang.Math; public class Pyramid extends Solid { private double length; private double width; private double height; public Pyramid(String name, double length, double width, double height) { super(name); this.length = length; this.width = width; this.height = height; } @Override public double volume() { return (length * width * height) / 3; } @Override public double surfaceArea() { double slantHeightL = Math.sqrt((length / 2) * (length / 2) + height * height); double slantHeightW = Math.sqrt((width / 2) * (width / 2) + height * height); double areaBase = length * width; double areaLateral = (length * slantHeightL + width * slantHeightW) / 2; return areaBase + areaLateral; } } ``` ### Cylinder Class The Cylinder class will represent a cylinder. ```java import java.lang.Math; public class Cylinder extends Solid { private double radius; private double height; public Cylinder(String name, double radius, double height) { super(name); this.radius = radius; this.height = height; } @Override public double volume() { return Math.PI * radius * radius * height; } @Override public double surfaceArea() { return 2 * Math.PI * radius * (radius + height); } } ``` ### RectangularPrism Class The RectangularPrism class will represent a rectangular prism. ```java public class RectangularPrism extends Solid { private double length; private double width; private double height; public RectangularPrism(String name, double length, double width, double height) { super(name); this.length = length; this.width = width; this.height = height; } @Override public double volume() { return length * width * height; } @Override public double surfaceArea() { return 2 * (length * width + length * height + width * height); } } ``` ### Sphere Class The Sphere class will represent a sphere. ```java import java.lang.Math; public class Sphere extends Solid { private double radius; public Sphere(String name, double radius) { super(name); this.radius = radius; } @Override public double volume() { return (4.0 / 3.0) * Math.PI * Math.pow(radius, 3); } @Override public double surfaceArea() { return 4 * Math.PI * Math.pow(radius, 2); } } ``` ### Cube Class The Cube class will represent a cube and extend RectangularPrism because a cube is a special type of rectangular prism where all sides are equal. ```java public class Cube extends RectangularPrism { public Cube(String name, double sideLength) { super(name, sideLength, sideLength, sideLength); } } ``` ### SolidTester Class The `SolidTester` class to test the implementations: ```java import java.util.ArrayList; public class SolidTester { public static void main(String[] args) { String name; double volume; double surfaceArea; // Pyramid constructor should take name, length, width, height in that order Pyramid pyramid = new Pyramid("My pyramid", 1, 3, 5); name = pyramid.getName(); volume = round(pyramid.volume(), 2); surfaceArea = round(pyramid.surfaceArea(), 2); System.out.println("Pyramid '" + name + "' has volume: " + volume + " and surface area: " + surfaceArea + "."); // Sphere constructor should take name then radius Sphere sphere = new Sphere("My sphere", 4); name = sphere.getName(); volume = round(sphere.volume(), 2); surfaceArea = round(sphere.surfaceArea(), 2); System.out.println("Sphere '" + name + "' has volume: " + volume + " and surface area: " + surfaceArea + "."); // RectangularPrism constructor should take name, length, width, height in that order RectangularPrism rectangularPrism = new RectangularPrism("My rectangular prism", 5, 8, 3); name = rectangularPrism.getName(); volume = round(rectangularPrism.volume(), 2); surfaceArea = round(rectangularPrism.surfaceArea(), 2); System.out.println("RectangularPrism '" + name + "' has volume: " + volume + " and surface area: " + surfaceArea + "."); // Cylinder constructor should take name, radius, height in that order Cylinder cylinder = new Cylinder("My cylinder", 4, 9); name = cylinder.getName(); volume = round(cylinder.volume(), 2); surfaceArea = round(cylinder.surfaceArea(), 2); System.out.println("Cylinder '" + name + "' has volume: " + volume + " and surface area: " + surfaceArea + "."); // Cube constructor should take name then side length in that order Cube cube = new Cube("My cube", 4); name = cube.getName(); volume = round(cube.volume(), 2); surfaceArea = round(cube.surfaceArea(), 2); System.out.println("Cube '" + name + "' has volume: " + volume + " and surface area: " + surfaceArea + "."); } public static double round(double value, int places) { if (places < 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException(); long factor = (long) Math.pow(10, places); value = value * factor; long tmp = Math.round(value); return (double) tmp / factor; } } ``` ### Summary - **Solid**: Base class with name, volume(), and surfaceArea() methods. - **Pyramid**: Extends Solid, calculates volume and surface area of a rectangular pyramid. - **Cylinder**: Extends Solid, calculates volume and surface area of a cylinder. - **RectangularPrism**: Extends Solid, calculates volume and surface area of a rectangular prism. - **Sphere**: Extends Solid, calculates volume and surface area of a sphere. - **Cube**: Extends RectangularPrism with equal sides. - **SolidTester**: Tests all the classes by creating instances and printing their volume and surface area.
